title: America's Classical Liberal Era
body:
## America's Classical Liberal Era

The Founders distilled English liberty into plain words:

- Government only stops harm to others
- Power divided so ambition checks ambition
- Property protected above all

For decades the federal government stayed tiny (<2% GDP). Result: from 13 colonies to the world's richest nation in record time.

Tocqueville marveled in 1831: Americans fixed problems themselves—churches, lodges, companies—not by begging the state.

Low taxes, sound money, open borders, minimal regulation = the greatest prosperity surge in history.

title: Bastiat's Government Paradox
body:
## Bastiat's Government Paradox

Bastiat's devastating one-liner (1850):

"The state is that great fiction by which everyone tries to live at the expense of everyone else."

And the perfect follow-up:

People say: "We don't want the government to grow grain."

Very well.

But then they add: "We want the government to feed the people, create jobs, give free healthcare, subsidise art, guarantee loans…"

It is as if they said:

"We do not want the government to grow grain—yet we demand that it magically provide bread for all."

Government has no resources of its own. It can only take from Peter to give to Paul.

Every "service" is paid for by someone who never consented.

Bastiat exposed the illusion in a single paragraph that still stings 175 years later.

title: Bastiat's Seen and Unseen
body:
## Bastiat's Seen and Unseen

In 1850 Frédéric Bastiat wrote the clearest parable in all economics:

A shopkeeper's son breaks a window.

The crowd says: "At least the glazier gets work. What's bad for the shopkeeper is good for trade!"

Bastiat replies:

That is what is **seen**.

What is **not seen** is the new suit the shopkeeper would have bought instead. The tailor loses work. Net result: society is poorer by one window and one suit.

Every government "stimulus," every regulation, every tax creates visible winners and invisible losers.

Politicians always point to the seen (jobs "created"). Classical liberals ask about the unseen (jobs destroyed, growth prevented).

Bastiat's one short essay destroys 99 % of bad economic thinking—then and now.

title: Edmund Burke Saves Freedom
body:
## Edmund Burke Saves Freedom

Edmund Burke supported the American Revolution and Catholic rights, but in 1790 he saw terror coming in France.

Revolutionaries were destroying church, monarchy, provinces, guilds—even the calendar—in the name of pure Reason.

Burke predicted the guillotine and Napoleon years before they happened. He was mocked, then proven right.

Freedom, he taught, grows slowly in real soil—manners, religion, property, custom. Rip it out and you get tyranny wearing liberty's mask.

Modern conservatism starts here.

title: Chile Miracle 1975–1990
body:
## Chile Miracle 1975–1990

1973 Chile under Allende: 600% inflation, empty shops, expropriations, Marxist chaos.

After Pinochet, the "Chicago Boys" launched radical free-market reforms:

- Privatized pensions, mines, utilities
- Flat 10% income tax
- Opened trade, slashed tariffs
- Independent central bank

1975–1990: average 7% annual growth. Poverty crashed from 45% to 15% by 2000.

Even left-wing governments after 1990 kept the model.

Chile leapt from socialist ruin to Latin America's richest, most stable economy—proof the formula works anywhere.

title: Corn Laws Repealed 1846
body:
## Corn Laws Repealed 1846

For decades the Corn Laws kept foreign grain out and bread prices high for the poor.

Cobden, Bright, and classical liberals fought. In 1846 Conservative PM Robert Peel finally repealed them.

The aristocracy predicted ruin.

Instead:

- Bread prices fell 25–40 %
- Real wages soared
- British farms grew richer on meat and dairy

Britain fed Europe while its own people doubled and prospered.

One vote proved free trade makes everyone richer.

title: Glorious Revolution 1688–89
body:
## Glorious Revolution 1688–89

In 1688 England stood on the edge of absolutist monarchy. James II was building a standing army, suspending laws by royal decree, and trying to re-impose Catholicism by force.

A group of bishops and nobles invited the Dutch prince William of Orange to invade and rescue English liberty.

William landed with a fleet larger than the Spanish Armada—yet almost no blood was shed. James II fled. Parliament passed the **English Bill of Rights (1689)**:

- No taxation without consent
- Free speech in Parliament
- No standing army in peacetime without Parliament's approval
- Right to petition the king
- Right of Protestants to bear arms

For the first time, a great nation declared that government exists to protect pre-existing rights. The state became a "night watchman." Classical Liberalism was born.

title: The Use of Knowledge in Society
grift: The Use of Knowledge in Society
body:
## The Use of Knowledge in Society

Published in the American Economic Review, September 1945.

This is the essay everyone quotes when they say "the knowledge-problem argument" or "Hayek's knowledge problem."

Key lines that changed economics forever:

- "The economic problem of society is not merely a problem of how to allocate 'given' resources… it is a problem of the **utilization of knowledge which is not given to anyone in its totality**."

- Most of the knowledge we need to coordinate a modern economy is **not** scientific data in textbooks.

It is local, fleeting, tacit:

- a factory manager knows his machines are wearing out

- a trader knows copper is suddenly scarce in one port

- a housewife knows she prefers more fruit this month

- No central planner can ever collect this dispersed, changing, often unarticulated knowledge.

- The price system solves it automatically: a higher copper price instantly tells thousands of producers and users around the world "economise or find substitutes" — without anyone needing to know _why_ the price rose.

Hayek's conclusion is devastating for central planning:

"The marvel is that in a case like that of the scarcity of one raw material, without an order being issued, without more than perhaps a few people knowing the cause, tens of thousands of people… are made to use the material or its products more sparingly; that is, they move in the right direction."

Mises showed socialism can't calculate.

Hayek showed that even if it somehow could, it still couldn't **discover** what needs to be calculated.

This 1945 essay (just 11 pages) is the single most cited and most lethal attack on central planning ever written.

title: Hayek's Road to Serfdom 1944
body:
## Hayek's Road to Serfdom 1944

In 1944, while the world fought Nazis and Soviets, Friedrich Hayek warned the West was walking the same path.

Central planning—even with good intentions—kills freedom.

Why? Plans need coercion. Someone must allocate resources. Dissent threatens the plan, so dissent is crushed.

Published in 1944, the book predicted nationalization → price controls → shortages → dictatorship.

Britain's 1945–51 Labour government followed the script almost perfectly.

Hayek proved socialism and freedom cannot coexist. A bestseller that later armed Thatcher, Reagan, and millions.

title: Hayek's Fatal Conceit 1988
body:
## Hayek's Fatal Conceit 1988

In 1988, at age 89, Friedrich Hayek dropped his final bombshell: _The Fatal Conceit: The Errors of Socialism_.

Socialism's core mistake is the belief that a few minds can consciously design a better order than the spontaneous order that billions create through free choices.

Civilisation is not the product of reason—it is the product of the extended order of markets, morals, language, law, and money that evolved over millennia.

This order is far wiser than any planner because it uses knowledge no single brain can hold: prices signal scarcity, traditions encode survival lessons, competition weeds out bad ideas.

To replace it with central planning is the height of arrogance—the "fatal conceit."

Hayek wrote it as Communism was visibly dying. Three years later the Soviet Union was gone.

The 20th century's greatest experiment in rational design ended in bread lines and mass graves. The unplanned order of liberty won again.

title: Liberalism Isn't dead - It's Buried Under 120 Years of Collectivist Garbage
grift: Liberalism Isn't dead - It's Buried Under 120 Years of Collectivist Garbage
body:
## Liberalism Isn’t dead, don't confuse it with Leftism.

![Declaration of Iindependence painting](/posts/declaration-of-independence-painting.jpg)

Liberalism is just buried under 120 years of collectivist garbage.

The core of liberalism is dead-simple:

- You own your life
- You own your body
- You own the fruits of your labor
- You get to speak, worship, assemble, and trade with whomever you want
- The government’s only job is to stop other people from violating those rights
- Equal rules for everyone, no special privileges for no one

That’s it. Everything else is commentary.

From roughly 1688 (Glorious Revolution) to 1914, the parts of the world that actually practiced this (Britain, the Netherlands, the United States, etc.) gave mankind the single greatest explosion of freedom, wealth, invention, and living standards in recorded history.

A very partial list of what freedom delivered in one century:

railroads · steamships · telegraph · telephone · electric light & power · recorded sound · photography · motion pictures · automobiles · airplanes · radio · X-rays · quantum physics · germ theory · anesthesia · antiseptics · the periodic table · skyscrapers · mass steel & aluminum · aspirin · diesel engines · indoor plumbing for the masses · real wages up 500–1000 % for ordinary workers · life expectancy from ~30 to ~70

Name another system that comes within an order of magnitude of that scorecard. I’ll wait.

Then the Marxists showed up and said “Equality before the law isn’t enough — you need enforced equality of outcome, and we’ll deliver it at gunpoint.”

Liberalism, because it actually believes in free speech and tolerance, let them talk.
**Big mistake.**

Gramsci told them to march through the institutions instead of the streets.
They did.

We laughed at the bearded weirdos in 1968.
Their students now run the universities, the NGOs, the HR departments, the media, and half the government bureaucracies.

We beat the USSR but imported the ideology anyway.

Today, the idea that the government should leave you alone is considered “far-right.”
That is how completely the Overton window has been dragged left.

In 1900, a 90 % top marginal tax rate and the state running your healthcare, education, and retirement would have been considered literal tyranny.
In 2025, suggesting we get rid of those things is considered literal tyranny.

That’s not “liberalism failed.”
That’s “we stopped being liberals and became soft-socialists while pretending we didn’t.”

And yeah, classical liberals in the 19th century were generally pro-free-migration because most countries still had small welfare states and tiny governments. Times have changed — massive vote-buying welfare states plus open borders is a suicide pact, not a liberal policy. So we adapt that part. The rest of the program is still perfect.

**Bottom line:**
I don’t defend liberalism because it created the wealthiest, healthiest, most innovative century in human history (even though it did).

I defend it because it is the **only system** that treats you like a free, adult human being instead of a toddler who needs the state to wipe your nose and plan your life.

Anyone arguing against maximum freedom is, whether they admit it or not, arguing for some flavor of imposed control.
That’s the Left, full stop.

**Freedom > everything else.**
And it just so happens the freest societies also became the richest, safest, and coolest.
Funny how that works.

Want the academic version?
Watch this:\
[Deirdre McCloskey – Why the 19th Century Boom Happened](https://youtu.be/a0nsKBx77EQ)

title: Magna Carta 1215
body:
## Magna Carta 1215

In 1215, English barons forced tyrannical King John to sign the Great Charter at Runnymede.

Key clauses:

- No taxation without consent
- No imprisonment without lawful judgment
- Justice cannot be sold or delayed

Originally for nobles, it became the seed of liberty: even the king is under the law.

Parliament cited it for centuries to expand habeas corpus, trial by jury, and limits on royal power. Classical liberals from Coke to Blackstone called it England's ancient constitution.

The first written curb on absolute power in European history—still law in 2025.

title: Mises Crushes Socialism 1922
body:
## Mises Crushes Socialism 1922

In 1922, when almost every intellectual believed scientific socialism was the future, Ludwig von Mises published _Socialism_ and delivered the knockout blow.

His argument was devastatingly simple:

Without private property in the means of production, there are no real market prices.

Without real prices, there is no way to calculate what anything costs or is worth.

Without economic calculation, rational production becomes impossible.

Socialism isn't evil—it is literally impossible. Central planners cannot do the math that millions of free people do automatically every day through buying and selling.

The Soviet Union spent seventy years proving Mises right: endless shortages, misallocated factories, tractors built with no wheels, mountains of unsold goods nobody wanted.

Mises fired the shot in 1922. By 1991 the entire socialist world was bankrupt and gone. The calculation argument won.

title: No Such Thing as Trickle-Down Economics
grift: No Such Thing as Trickle-Down Economics
body:
## There is No Such Thing as Trickle-Down Economics.

"In addition, those of us who support such [so-called Trickle down] policies don’t want to “give” anything to anyone, whether rich or poor. When people talk about tax cuts as “giving” something to someone, they implicitly start from the premise that everything belongs to government and we are only able to keep some for ourselves by its indulgence of us." - Steven Horwitz

[FEE article - There is No Such Thing as Trickle-Down Economics](https://fee.org/articles/there-is-no-such-thing-as-trickle-down-economics/ 'There is No Such Thing as Trickle-Down Economics')

title: Reagan Revives America 1981
body:
## Reagan Revives America 1981

1980 America: 13% inflation, 21% interest rates, 7% unemployment, "malaise," hostages in Iran.

Reagan slashed top tax from 70% to 28%, deregulated energy, airlines, trucking, and backed Volcker's war on inflation.

1983–1989 results:

- 21 million new jobs
- Inflation down to 3%
- Longest peacetime expansion ever
- USSR bankrupted trying to match American growth

"Reaganomics" was classical liberalism on the world stage: lower taxes, sound money, fewer rules = explosive prosperity.

title: Scottish Miracle 1707-1850
body:
## Scottish Miracle 1707-1850

In 1700 Scotland was one of Europe's poorest nations—regular famines, almost no industry, life expectancy under 40.

Then everything changed:

- 1707 Union opened England's markets
- Scottish Enlightenment (Hume, Smith) explained wealth
- Merchants unleashed industry

Result: GDP per person grew **fifteen-fold** in a century—the fastest leap ever recorded. Literacy hit 90%. A nation of shepherds gave the world the steam engine and modern economics.

Capitalism didn't need theories; Scotland lived it.

title: Spontaneous Order Triumphs
body:
## Spontaneous Order Triumphs

Markets, language, law, money, morals—no central planner designed any of them.

They emerged as spontaneous orders: billions of individual actions, guided by simple rules, producing results no one intended and no one could plan.

Adam Smith's "invisible hand," Hayek's "extended order," Ferguson's "the result of human action, but not of human design."

The pencil you hold: no one on earth knows how to make it from scratch—yet millions cooperate seamlessly through prices and property.

Cities grow without a master architect. The internet exploded without a ministry of information.

Every attempt to replace spontaneous order with conscious design—Soviet five-year plans, Mao's backyard furnaces, Venezuela's price controls—ends in chaos and misery.

Freedom doesn't need a blueprint. It just needs to be left alone.

title: Thatcher Turns Britain 1979
body:
## Thatcher Turns Britain 1979

1979 Britain: 29% inflation, unburied dead, three-day work week, IMF bailout—the "sick man of Europe."

Margaret Thatcher applied classical liberalism with an iron will:

- Sold council houses to tenants
- Broke inflationary union power
- Privatized steel, coal, telecoms, airlines
- Cut top tax rate from 83% to 40%

By 1990: inflation <3%, unemployment falling, GDP growth doubled, home ownership soared.

Britain went from basket case to Europe's strongest economy.

Proof that liberal policies rescue even deeply socialist nations.

title: The Long March Through the Institutions: A Slow-Motion Cultural Coup
grift: The Long March Through the Institutions: A Slow-Motion Cultural Coup
body:
## The Long March Through the Institutions: A Slow-Motion Cultural Coup

After World War II, many European Marxist intellectuals realized that the old dream of a violent proletarian revolution (storming the Winter Palace, guillotine-the-bourgeoisie style) was dead. The working class in the West had been bought off with refrigerators, cars, and Sunday barbecues. Armed uprising wasn’t going to happen.

Enter the German student radical **Rudi Dutschke** in 1967. Borrowing directly from the Italian communist **Antonio Gramsci’s** theories of cultural hegemony, Dutschke popularized the phrase **“the long march through the institutions”**. The plan was simple but diabolical:

> Instead of trying to seize power by force (which would get you crushed by the state), you infiltrate and capture the institutions that shape culture and consciousness: universities, schools, media, publishing houses, churches, the civil service, HR departments, museums, the arts, and eventually even corporate boardrooms.

You do it patiently, generation after generation, by becoming the professors, the journalists, the teachers, the scriptwriters, the diversity officers, the curators. You change the language, rewrite the history books, shift what is considered “normal,” and make your once-radical ideas the new compulsory orthodoxy. Anyone who objects is labeled a bigot, a reactionary, or—today’s favorite—a “fascist.”

The **Frankfurt School** (Marcuse, Adorno, Horkheimer, Habermas, et al.) had already laid the intellectual groundwork in the 1930s–50s, blending Marx with Freud and a deep hatred of Western civilization itself. When they fled Nazi Germany and landed in American universities (especially Columbia), they began the infection in earnest. By the late 60s their students—now tenured—were running entire departments.

## What followed was a fifty-year conquest:

- **1970s**: “Political correctness” starts as an in-joke on the left, then becomes mandatory speech.
- **1980s**: Critical theory metastasizes into Women’s Studies, Black Studies, Queer Studies—entire academic fields built not to pursue truth but to advance ideological warfare.
- **1990s**: The media class graduates from the same universities and brings the new dogma into newsrooms and Hollywood.
- **2000s–2010s**: HR departments and “diversity & inclusion” become the corporate Stasi, enforcing the new secular religion.
- **2020s**: Even kindergarten curricula, library story hours, and scientific bodies are captured. Dissent is not just wrong—it is morally illegitimate, worthy of cancellation, firing, deplatforming.

The result? A civilization that no longer believes in itself, that teaches its children to be ashamed of their history, that polices every word and thought, that replaced individual merit with bureaucratic ideology, and that now struggles to even define “woman” without a 400-page DEI manual.

**The Long March worked.** The revolutionaries never needed rifles. They just needed tenure, microphones, and a lot of patience.

And the scariest part: most people still don’t realize the institutions have already been marched through. They think it’s just “progress.”

**Wake Up NPC, indeed.**
